summary: 20th-century English poet known for 'This Be the Verse,' 'An Arundel Tomb,' 'Church Going,' and 'The Whitsun Weddings.' University of Hull librarian. Member of The Movement.

works.name: "This Be the Verse"
works.description: Larkin's most quoted poem, opening 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad.' 'They fill you with the faults they had / And add some extra, just for you.' Describes 'fools in old-style hats and coats' who were 'at one another's throats.' 'Man hands on misery to man. / It deepens like a coastal shelf.' Ends: 'Get out as early as you can, / And don't have any kids yourself.' Title from Robert Louis Stevenson's poem 'Requiem' ('This be the verse you grave for me').

clues.clue: 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad'
examples: wrote 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad'

clues.clue: 'Man hands on misery to man. / It deepens like a coastal shelf'
examples: wrote a poem describing how 'man hands on misery to man'

clues.clue: 'Get out as early as you can, / And don't have any kids yourself'
examples: advising the reader to not 'have any kids yourself'
This author used a line from Robert Louis Stevenson's poem 'Requiem' to title a poem

works.name: "An Arundel Tomb"
works.description: Describes an earl and countess who 'lie in stone' in Chichester Cathedral. 'Their faces blurred.' 'Such plainness of the pre-Baroque hardly involves the eye.' 'Time has transfigured them into / Untruth.' A 'sharp tender shock' upon seeing the earl's hand still clasping his wife's. Concludes with the 'almost-instinct almost truth' that 'What will survive of us is love.' Inspired by Larkin's visit to Chichester with Monica Jones.

clues.clue: 'What will survive of us is love' — 'almost-instinct almost truth'; earl and countess 'lie in stone'
examples: 'What will survive of us is love'
concludes with an 'almost-instinct almost truth'
'time has transfigured them into Untruth'

clues.clue: 'such faithfulness in effigy / was just a detail friends would see'; 'sharp tender shock'
examples: experiences a 'sharp tender shock' upon seeing a stone earl's hand still clasping his wife's

works.name: "Church Going"
works.description: Speaker visits an empty church, donates an Irish sixpence, reads 'Here endeth' much too loudly. Removes his 'cycle-clips in awkward reverence.' Describes a 'shape less recognizable each week, a purpose more obscure.' Wonders 'shall we avoid them as unlucky places?' and 'what remains when disbelief has gone?' Calls it 'A serious house on serious earth.' Considers 'dubious women' who 'make their children touch a particular stone.' Called religion a 'vast moth-eaten musical brocade.'

clues.clue: Donates Irish sixpence; reads 'Here endeth' too loudly; 'cycle-clips in awkward reverence'; 'serious house on serious earth'
examples: donates an Irish sixpence and recalls proclaiming 'Here endeth' too loudly
'A serious house on serious earth'

clues.clue: 'what remains when disbelief has gone?'; 'vast moth-eaten musical brocade'
examples: wondered, 'what remains when disbelief has gone?'
called religion a 'vast moth-eaten musical brocade'

works.name: "The Whitsun Weddings"
works.description: Title poem of a collection. Speaker rides a 'three-quarters-empty train' on a sunlit Saturday, passing bridal parties at stations. Sees 'girls in parodies of fashion, heels and veils,' 'fathers with broad belts,' 'mothers loud and fat.' 'A sense of falling, like an arrow-shower / Sent out of sight, somewhere becoming rain.' Fifty minutes is 'just enough time to settle hats and say I nearly died.' Smells a fish market.

clues.clue: 'three-quarters-empty train'; bridal parties; 'girls in parodies of fashion'; 'arrow-shower becoming rain'
examples: he is riding a 'three-quarters-empty train'
'A sense of falling, like an arrow-shower / Sent out of sight, somewhere becoming rain'

works.name: "High Windows"
works.description: Title poem of a collection. 'I see a couple of kids and guess he's fucking her and she's taking pills or wearing a diaphragm.' 'I know this is paradise.' Imagines 'everyone young going down the long slide to happiness, endlessly.' Ends with 'the deep blue air, that shows / Nothing, and is nowhere, and is endless.' Wonders if earlier generations saw his as 'free bloody birds.'

clues.clue: 'everyone young going down the long slide to happiness'; 'the deep blue air that shows nothing, and is nowhere, and is endless'
examples: 'everyone young going down the long slide to happiness, endlessly'
'the deep blue air, that shows nothing, and is nowhere, and is endless'

works.name: "Aubade"
works.description: Opens 'I work all day, and get half-drunk at night.' 'Waking at four to soundless dark.' 'Death is no different whined at than withstood.' Death described as 'The anaesthetic from which none come round.' 'Postmen like doctors go from house to house.' Parodies the medieval French aubade form. Les Murray parodied it.

clues.clue: 'I work all day, and get half-drunk at night'; 'Death is no different whined at than withstood'; 'postmen like doctors'
examples: begins: 'I work all day, and get half-drunk at night'
'Death is not different whined at than withstood'

works.name: Other Poems and Biographical
works.description: Other poems: 'Annus Mirabilis' ('Sexual intercourse began / In nineteen sixty-three / Between the end of the Chatterley ban / And the Beatles' first LP'). 'Mr Bleaney': rents a room of the deceased title character; 'how we live measures our own nature.' 'Faith Healing': 'immense slackening ache.' 'Toads': 'why should I let the toad work squat on my life.' 'Talking in Bed': 'a unique distance from isolation.' 'Dublinesque': funeral procession, voice 'singing of Kitty, or Katy.' 'Sad Steps': drawn from Philip Sidney. 'MCMXIV': 'archaic faces grinning'; 'never such innocence.' 'Days': 'the priest and the doctor / In their long coats / running over the fields.' 'Afternoons': 'courting-places that are still courting-places.' 'Vers de Societe': Warlock-Williams's invitation. 'For Sidney Bechet': 'New Orleans reflected on the water.' First novel Jill: John Kemp creates an imaginary woman. Larkin was a librarian at the University of Hull. Member of The Movement with Kingsley Amis. His posthumous letters revealed casual racism. Made significant contributions to Amis's Lucky Jim. Wrote pseudonymous girls school novels as Brunette Coleman. Turned down Poet Laureate; Ted Hughes took it. Close friends with John Betjeman. Lover Monica Jones.

clues.clue: 'Annus Mirabilis': sexual intercourse began between 'end of the Chatterley ban / And the Beatles' first LP'
examples: 'every life became / a brilliant breaking of the bank' after 'the end of the Chatterley ban'

clues.clue: Librarian at University of Hull; member of The Movement with Kingsley Amis
examples: This librarian and poet
This English poet of 'High Windows' was a prominent member of the Movement
This poet's posthumous reputation suffered in 1992, after Anthony Thwaite published an edition of their collected letters replete with casual racism

clues.clue: 'Mr Bleaney': rents room of deceased; 'no more to show / Than one hired box'
examples: spoke about a man from 'up by Kosciusko's side' who wrote about how 'one-hired box should make him pretty sure / He warranted better'

clues.clue: Turned down Poet Laureate; Ted Hughes accepted
examples: Elizabeth II offered Larkin the post of poet laureate, but he turned it down

comprehensive_summary:
Philip Larkin is a 20th-century English poet whose giveaway is invariably 'This Be the Verse,' opening with 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad.' The poem describes how 'Man hands on misery to man' and advises 'don't have any kids yourself.' Its title comes from Stevenson's 'Requiem.'

'An Arundel Tomb' describes an earl and countess in Chichester Cathedral with the famous concluding line 'What will survive of us is love,' qualified as an 'almost-instinct almost truth.' 'Time has transfigured them into / Untruth.' 'Church Going' depicts visiting an empty church, donating an Irish sixpence, reading 'Here endeth' too loudly, and reflecting on what happens when belief dies — calling the building 'A serious house on serious earth.'

'The Whitsun Weddings' describes a 'three-quarters-empty train' ride past bridal parties, ending with 'an arrow-shower becoming rain.' 'High Windows' imagines 'everyone young going down the long slide to happiness' and ends with 'the deep blue air that shows nothing.' 'Aubade' opens 'I work all day, and get half-drunk at night' and confronts death as 'The anaesthetic from which none come round.'

Other key poems include 'Annus Mirabilis' (sexual intercourse began between 'the Chatterley ban / And the Beatles' first LP'), 'Mr Bleaney,' 'Toads,' and 'Dublinesque.' Larkin was a librarian at the University of Hull and a member of The Movement with Kingsley Amis. He turned down the Poet Laureateship (Ted Hughes accepted). His posthumous letters revealed casual racism. He was close to John Betjeman and his lover was Monica Jones.
